mechanism_description: Diverting non-recycled waste to waste-to-energy facilities reduces landfill methane while generating energy that can displace more carbon-intensive power sources.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Converting residual waste into usable electricity/heat recovers energy value from materials that would otherwise be discarded, delivering the same energy services with less virgin fuel input.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste management choice (landfill → WtE) → ↓ anaerobic decomposition in landfills → ↓ CH₄ emissions (plus energy recovery → ↓ fossil generation) → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in system/activity: Municipal solid waste that would be landfilled is instead processed in waste-to-energy facilities (e.g., combustion, gasification, anaerobic digestion of suitable fractions) and used to produce electricity/heat.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced landfill deposition lowers anaerobic biodegradation that produces methane; simultaneously, recovered energy supplies the grid/loads and can substitute for fossil-based generation.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions, primarily via avoided methane and secondarily via displaced fossil CO₂ (net of facility emissions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering net GHG emissions by avoiding landfill methane from disposed waste (often the dominant portion of the benefit), with additional mitigation from energy recovery displacing fossil generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Waste diversion from landfill to WtE reduces methane formation and yields energy that displaces higher-carbon generation → net ↓ GHG.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The intended and largest climate effect is emissions mitigation (CH₄ avoidance and fossil displacement), not hazard reduction (adaptation) or ecosystem carbon (NBS).  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism explicitly recovers usable energy from residual waste, reducing demand for other primary energy inputs for the same delivered electricity/heat.  
4) Edge cases applied: Even where WtE has CO₂ emissions, the canonical mechanism specified includes landfill methane avoidance and energy recovery; the climate framing remains mitigation, though net benefit depends on waste composition, baseline landfill gas capture, and displaced grid intensity.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy implementing “waste-to-energy conversion → landfill methane avoidance and energy recovery” necessarily targets GHG reduction (mitigation) and inherently recovers energy from waste (resource efficiency).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net mitigation magnitude can flip or shrink where (a) landfills already have very high methane capture and utilization, (b) displaced electricity is already low-carbon, or (c) high-plastics waste increases fossil CO₂ from combustion; some implementations may also create local air-pollution tradeoffs, but those do not change the primary climate category.
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced landfill volume/land demand; potential local energy resilience; reduced landfill leachate risk (not explicitly stated and not clearly tied to Miami-Dade’s listed hazards in the text)
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type is inferred because the source provides only an “approach” title with no explicit policy tool; alternative plausible instrument types (e.g., target-setting or regulatory-mandate for diversion) are not evidenced in the verbatim text.
